Stop using 'cultural fit' as an excuse to hire the same people over and over again. When every person in your tech team thinks exactly the same, you haven't built a defence—you've built a massive, predictable blind spot.

Welcome to a special DTX Edition of the SECURE | CYBER CONNECT Podcast. Justin (Jay) Adamson and I are thrilled to be joined by Kayla Vondy -a leader operating at the high-stakes intersection of government policy and cybersecurity- exposes why the industry’s obsession with 'standardised' talent is a national security risk. We are stripping away the red tape to look at the raw realities of 2026 public sector tech, from the 'Immigration Tax' to the danger of neurotypical-only threat modelling.

🔥 WHAT YOU'LL DISCOVER:
•  🧠 The Bureaucratic Blind Spot: Why neurotypical-only teams are fundamentally incapable of effective threat modelling against non-linear attackers.
•  🚨 The Immigration Tax: The silent, exhausting pressure of proving your worth in UK tech while navigating unwritten cultural rules and visa dynamics.
•  🎭 The Weaponisation of 'Cultural Fit': How HR terminology is actively used as a gatekeeper to keep diverse, brilliant minds out of the room.
•  🤖 AI for the Social Contract: Building 'human-first resilience' for vulnerable citizens who rely on government systems every day.
• 📍 DTX Special- Purpose to Practice: How to move beyond policy checkboxes to actual citizen-facing delivery in 2026.
•  ⚡ The Risk Paradox: How neurodivergent leaders bridge the gap between 'fail fast' tech culture and 'never fail' government bureaucracy.
•  🛒 Procurement Red Flags: The one question every government buyer must ask vendors to stop procuring 'exclusionary' tech.
•  🛠️ The 2026 Playbook: Which 'unwritten rule' of government tech must be deleted to make the industry work for everyone.

🔹 Kayla Vondy - Kayla is an AI Project Manager at the Liverpool City Region Combined Authority, where she works across AI, cybersecurity, and public sector delivery. She brings deep expertise from the tech and cyber fields alongside her lived experience as a neurodiverse woman. A regular speaker on responsible AI and digital inclusion, Kayla is dedicated to building inclusive, resilient systems that work for real people.

As agentic AI takes over routine development and maintenance work, companies must fundamentally redesign their technology workforce. 

The end goal is to generate real ROI from AI—but getting the formula right is complex. 
CEOs face mounting pressures: cost reduction, geopolitical risk, shifting vendor value, and new workforce dynamics. 
Success requires rebalancing three interconnected levers—hiring, internal capability building, and vendor strategy. With the right balance, companies can triple the return they get from their technology investments. CEOs who act decisively will shape long-term economics; those who wait risk being locked into inflexible structures.

1. Hire fewer, more selective technologists to maximize ROI per employee

· Demand shifts from junior developers to senior engineers and architects who can orchestrate work across humans, agents, and vendors.
· Expert technologists using AI are several times more productive than less experienced peers, with a modest pay differential relative to the output gap.
· ROI implication: Hiring for volume rather than expertise inflates costs without increasing impact. Fewer, deliberate hires deliver more value.

2. Build internal capabilities that AI cannot replace—or risk losing ROI to vendors

· Companies cannot outsource their understanding of AI’s impact on road maps, platforms, data, or architecture without losing control of value creation.
· Adopt a skills-based view. Map proficiency, identify critical talent, and invest systematically.
· ROI implication: Company’s disciplined skills approach results in tens of millions of money in new value creation over a few years.

3. Renegotiate vendor relationships for outcomes, not effort—to capture ROI directly

· Move from managed services to managed value. Link outsourced contracts to delivery speed, quality, and business impact, not head count.
· Shift from seat-based SaaS to API-first, outcome-linked models.
· ROI implication: A bank introduced AI-driven automation targets achieve a 10% productivity uplift across internal and external teams.

4. Lead change management to unlock ROI from human-agent teams

· The shift is not purely technological. Employees need support to navigate evolving roles. Without top-down change management, reskilling efforts remain ineffective.
· ROI implication: Successful companies infuse agentic AI throughout the organization to create a true hybrid workforce—turning every employee into a technologist and empowering human-agent teams to deliver AI at scale.

5. Integrate hiring, upskilling, and vendor strategy as one system to triple returns

· These levers reinforce each other. Taken together, they shape the economics of technology in an AI-first organization.
· ROI implication: With the right balance, companies can ultimately triple the return they get from their technology investments, rather than settling for short-lived efficiency gains.

⚡️ EU AI Act: IT companies face fines up to €35M — what changes in August 2026 and who is affected

Starting August 2, 2026, the strict rules for high-risk AI systems under the EU AI Act will fully apply. While the regulation entered into force in August 2024, this is the key deadline when most high-risk requirements become mandatory.

**What is considered high-risk AI?**
These are systems that can significantly impact people’s lives — especially in employment (Annex III):

🟢 CV screening and filtering
🟢 Candidate evaluation
🟢 Hiring recommendations
🟢 Automated decisions on hiring, promotion, task allocation, or termination
🟢 Employee performance monitoring

If your AI influences access to jobs — it is high-risk.

**Who does this apply to?**
The regulation is based on *where AI is used*, not where your company is registered:

🟢 SaaS products with AI features used or sold in the EU
🟢 Outsourcing teams building/supporting AI for EU clients
🟢 Companies whose customers use their product in Europe
🟢 Startups planning EU market entry
🟢 Any AI usage involving EU data or EU candidates/employees

**Key risks**

🟢 Using AI in HR processes without proper oversight
🟢 Applying third-party tools (e.g. ChatGPT) without policies and validation
🟢 Lack of technical documentation (model logic, training data, bias testing)
🟢 Fully automated decisions without human oversight
🟢 Lack of transparency: inability to explain decisions

**Fines are serious:**

🔴 Up to €35M or 7% of global turnover — for the most severe violations
🔴 Up to €15M or 3% — for breaches of high-risk requirements

This looks a lot like GDPR: many ignored it at first — until EU clients made compliance a non-negotiable contract requirement.

**What to do now (checklist)**

✔️ Inventory — identify where AI is used (products, internal processes, HR)
✔️ Risk assessment — does AI affect decisions about people? Any EU connection?
✔️ Documentation — model behavior, training data, bias testing, risk management
✔️ Human oversight — ensure final decisions are made by humans
✔️ Transparency — inform candidates and employees about AI usage
✔️ Policies — define internal rules for generative AI usage
✔️ Audit & monitoring — plan ongoing checks and post-deployment monitoring

The earlier you start, the smoother August 2026 will be. Many EU clients are already including AI Act compliance in contracts.

If your company works with the EU — this is no longer “something that might affect you.” It already does.

Those who make compliance part of their product will gain a real competitive advantage.

The best healthcare employee scheduling strategy during a shortage combines flexible, self-service models with predictive, data-driven technology to maximize existing staff, prevent burnout, and reduce reliance on expensive agency personnel. The goal is to move from rigid, manual rosters to dynamic scheduling that allows staff to choose shifts, trade easily, and align with real-time patient demand. 

Top Scheduling Strategies During a Shortage:
-Self-Scheduling: Allowing nurses to choose their own shifts within set parameters (e.g., skill mix,, core staffing requirements) significantly boosts engagement, retention, and satisfaction, particularly among Gen Z and Millennial workers.
-Internal Float Pools & Cross-Training: Building an internal "rapid response" team of cross-trained nurses who can work across multiple units allows for real-time reallocation based on patient acuity, reducing the need for external travel nurses.
-On-Demand/Mobile Scheduling Apps: Using technology like NurseGrid, Deputy, or OnShift enables staff to view open shifts, claim them in real-time, and trade shifts instantly. This automation fills gaps faster than manual texting and reduces the administrative burden of last-minute call-outs.
-Staggered/Variable Shifts: Moving away from standard 12-hour shifts to varied start times (e.g., 4-hour, 8-hour, or hybrid remote shifts) allows for better coverage during peak hours and supports work-life balance.
-Predictive Demand-Based Scheduling: Implementing AI-powered tools that analyze historical data (patient census, call-outs, season) to forecast staffing needs ensures you are not overstaffing slow days or understaffing busy ones. 

Best Practices for Implementation:
-Prioritize Retention: Before hiring, fix the burnout issue. Effective scheduling includes ensuring adequate time off between shifts and avoiding consecutive long shifts.
-"Float First" Model: Exhaust all internal options (float pools, extra shifts, part-time staff) before resorting to expensive contract or agency labor.
-Utilize Technology for Compliance: Use software that automatically alerts managers to potential violations, such as overtime, fatigue rules, or expired credentials.
-Offer Flexible Contracts: Provide per diem, weekend-only, or "part-time plus" roles to attract nurses who cannot work traditional full-time hours. 

Recommended Technology Solutions
-QGenda: Excellent for AI-driven, predictive scheduling and enterprise-level workforce management.
-Shiftboard: Strong for real-time adjustments and auto-blocking scheduling violations.
-NurseGrid: Highly regarded by nursing staff for self-scheduling and shift-swapping mobile functionality.
-Teambridge/Deputy: Effective for smaller to mid-sized practices needing flexible, AI-powered scheduling.

The 2026 AI Cheat Sheet for the C-Suite.

AI isn't one thing. It's five layers,  and each one does something completely different for your business.

If you're an executive, save this so you're investing in the right layer for the right problem:

1. Spots Patterns What it does: Finds trends hiding in your numbers. 
Where it helps: Sales forecasts, fraud detection, spotting who's about to churn.

2. Recognizes Things What it does: Sees, hears, and reads stuff. 
Where it helps: Scanning documents, voice-to-text, reading handwritten forms.

3. Creates New Stuff What it does: Writes, draws, and makes things from scratch. 
Where it helps: Drafting emails, writing product descriptions, generating images and code.

4. Does Tasks For You What it does: Finishes whole jobs on its own. 
Where it helps: Booking travel, scheduling meetings, reviewing contracts, onboarding new hires.

5. Runs Whole Workflows What it does: Runs entire projects end-to-end, with minimal oversight. 
Where it helps: Marketing campaigns, hiring pipelines, compliance audits, closing the books.

The jump from Layer 3 (creating stuff) to Layer 5 (running whole workflows) is the biggest shift of the decade.

Most companies are stuck at Layer 3. 
The winners in 2026 will be the ones who figure out Layer 5.

3 ways fractional GCs can scale to 10+ clients without burning out

Using public LLMs for client work isn't "innovation"; it's a malpractice risk.

I don't say that to be dramatic. I say it because I've spent months talking to fGCs who don't realize what they're doing.

Here's the problem: when you paste a client contract into ChatGPT, that data doesn't disappear. It moves. It trains. It sits somewhere you don't control. For a fractional GC managing five or six clients simultaneously, that's not a minor concern — that's a confidentiality nightmare waiting to happen.

I built Sapphire around a different assumption: client data sovereignty is non-negotiable, not a premium feature.

That meant building with tenant isolation from day one — each client's data is walled off, not just logically separated but architecturally isolated. It meant thinking hard about model hallucination mitigation, because a lawyer acting on a confident-but-wrong AI output is a real liability, not a theoretical one.

Most AI tools are built for speed. I'm building for trust.

The fGCs I talk to aren't looking to cut corners. They're trying to scale a serious practice without hiring a full team. The tools they use need to meet that standard.

Public AI doesn't.

The question I keep sitting with: why is "private by default" still treated as optional in legal tech?

Zapier tested AI interviews on their own hiring process before selling it to anyone else. That one detail matters more than anything else.

Most companies buying AI recruiting tools right now are buying from people who have never recruited a single human being. They built something technically impressive, demoed it well, and now your candidates are the beta test.

The number of ways an interview can go sideways is enormous. Context matters. Tone matters. The weird pause where someone is deciding whether to be honest matters.

So when I see a team that actually used their own tool on real roles with real stakes before shipping it, I pay attention. That is rare.

The German Mittelstand (SMEs) is slowly warming up to AI in hiring. Good. But please, before you sign a contract with any vendor, ask one question: have you used this on your own open roles?

If the answer is no, or a long explanation that sounds like yes but isn't, keep walking.

Your candidates are not a testing environment.

A conversation that happens recently.

A founder needs a VP of Data. Series A, eighteen months post-raise, team of three analysts and no real data infrastructure. They know what they need. They also know, quietly, that they can't yet build the environment that person deserves to walk into.

Then a senior data leader is ready for their next move. Fifteen years of experience. Led functions at two scale-ups. Doesn't want another role where they spend the first year fighting for budget and the next year wondering if the mandate was ever real.

Both of them are describing the same problem from opposite ends.
And increasingly, the answer sitting between them is fractional leadership.
I used to think of fractional as a compromise. The thing you did when the full-time hire wasn't possible yet. A placeholder with a polite name.
I've changed my mind.

What I'm seeing across the UK and Scandinavian AI startup market is something different. Experienced data and AI leaders choosing fractional deliberately — not because they can't get a full-time role, but because they've had full-time roles and they understand what they're trading away. Autonomy. Breadth. The ability to work across problems that actually interest them rather than the one problem a single company needs them to stay inside.

And founders at Series A and B who aren't settling for fractional — they're using it strategically. Bringing in a Head of Data two days a week to build the foundation before the team exists to support a full hire. Getting the thinking right before committing to the person.

The model only breaks when both sides treat it as temporary.

When it's chosen with intention — by a leader who wants this and a company that's ready to use it well — it produces some of the most focused, high-impact work I've seen at this stage of a company's growth.

The executive hiring market is changing. The leaders who understand that a title and a full-time contract are not the same thing as impact are already ahead of it.

Some good research on AI use in hiring assessments.

There’s a lot of noise in this space at the moment.

This paper is one of the first to look at what candidates are actually doing in real assessments.

A few findings stood out:

1️⃣ AI use is rising quickly, but still uneven

In 2024, fewer than 3% of candidates reported using GenAI.

By 2025, that number had jumped to over 20% in some groups.

This isn’t a future problem. It’s an adoption curve that’s already started.

2️⃣ Most candidates aren’t “cheating the whole test”

AI use was typically limited to a small number of responses.

The most common reasons were:

◾ clarifying instructions
◾ dealing with cognitively difficult questions

That suggests something more nuanced than wholesale cheating.

It’s often a response to uncertainty and task difficulty.

3️⃣ Cognitive tests are more exposed than personality

Around two-thirds of AI use was on problem-solving questions.

Very little was used on personality measures.

Different assessment methods are not equally vulnerable.

That matters for how we design selection processes.

4️⃣ Simple warnings actually work

A short pre-assessment warning roughly halved reported AI use.

No complex tech. No proctoring.

Just a clear signal about expectations and consequences.

Importantly, there was no negative impact on candidate experience.

Which is exactly why this matters in practice.

At Clevry, we take a layered approach rather than relying on a single control:

- upfront honesty contracts to set expectations
- clear warnings before and during the assessment
- no copy/paste or screenshots
- alerts if candidates click away from the screen
- flagging behaviour that diverges from these parameters

Because the reality is, this isn’t about catching people out.

It’s about reducing temptation, setting norms, and protecting the integrity of the data.

AI in assessment isn’t going away.

But neither is good measurement if you design for it properly.

Credit to Minerva Psychology for bringing this research to my attention.

Most contractors do not have a hiring problem.

They have a starting problem.

The worker shows up. No one is ready for them. Induction done on the fly. 
Contract sorted three days later. Hours tracked on a scrap of paper.

They clock it immediately. And they are gone by week two.

You have seen it. Probably caused it once or twice.

In 2026 it also carries legal risk. No written record means no protection when something goes wrong.

Fix it before the next hire:

• Worker verified before day one
• Contract and hours in writing before they start
• CIS confirmed, not assumed
• Shifts logged from week one
• Apprentice incentives claimed correctly (up to £5,000 available, most SMEs never apply)

One page. Free. Built for UK contractors.

One messy hire in 2026 could cost more than you made on the job. 
Comment "CHECKLIST" before your next hire starts.

*AMERICA SPENT $285 BILLION… AND MAY STILL LOSE THE AI WAR*
A striking paradox is emerging in the global AI race: unprecedented capital, unmatched infrastructure—and a rapidly weakening talent pipeline.
A recent report by Stanford University lays out a reality that should concern policymakers, investors, and technologists alike.

1️⃣ The Collapse of the Talent Magnet
The United States, long the global hub for AI talent, has seen an 89% collapse in net inflow of AI researchers since 2017
Even more alarming: 80% of this decline happened in just the last 12 months
This is the same ecosystem that created the transformer architecture and gave rise to:
OpenAI
Anthropic
Google DeepMind
xAI
👉 Capital is flowing. Talent is not.
2️⃣ The $100,000 Visa Barrier
The cost of hiring foreign AI talent via H-1B visas has reportedly surged to $100,000 per hire
Up from $2,000–$5,000 earlier, this is not a cost increase—it is a structural deterrent
Consider the implications:
A Chinese postdoc from Tsinghua University → $100K entry ticket
A French ML engineer → $100K
An Indian PhD critical to your model → $100K
👉 This isn’t friction. This is a talent blockade.
3️⃣ China Is Closing the Gap—Fast
Performance gap between top US and Chinese models has narrowed to ~2.7 percentage points
Down from 20+ points just two years ago
Since early 2025, leadership has changed hands multiple times
China now leads in:
AI publications
AI patents
Industrial robot deployment
Meanwhile, the US ranks 24th in AI adoption, behind countries like:
United Arab Emirates
Singapore
👉 Innovation without adoption is not dominance.
4️⃣ The Talent Equation Has Flipped
Nearly 50% of the world’s top AI researchers are Chinese, as noted by Jensen Huang
For two decades, the US playbook was simple:
Attract global talent
Retain them with opportunity and incentives
Build the future on imported intelligence
That model is now breaking.
AI labs are quietly expanding outside the US:
Zurich is emerging as a new hub
European and Asian ecosystems are gaining ground
👉 Talent goes where it is welcomed—not where it is restricted.
5️⃣ The Capex Illusion
The US is investing $285+ billion annually in AI
Massive bets on:
Data centers
GPUs (led by companies like NVIDIA)
Cloud contracts
Energy infrastructure
But here’s the uncomfortable truth:
AI is not a hardware race. It is a human capital race.
Without the right minds:
Chips are idle
Data is underutilized
Models stagnate
👉 You cannot compute your way out of a talent deficit.
6️⃣ The Strategic Risk Ahead
If the US loses its AI lead, it won’t be because:
China had better algorithms
Or more capital
Or superior hardware
It will be because:
It restricted the very talent that built its advantage
The loss will not happen in a lab.

The global AI race is entering a new phase:
From capital dominance → talent competition
From innovation → execution and adoption
From openness → strategic control
Nations that understand this shift will lead
